concepts.title: Rolle's Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
Rolle's Theorem

Statement: If a function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is

(i) continuous in the closed interval $\left[ a,b \right]$ i.e. continuous at each point in the interval $\left[ a,b \right]$.

(ii) differentiable in an open interval $\left( a,b \right)$ i.e. differentiable at each point in the open interval $\left( \text{a},\text{b} \right)$ and

(iii) $f\left( a \right)=f\left( b \right)$, then there will be at least one point $c$, in the interval $\left( a,b \right)$ such that ${f}'\left( c \right)=0$.

 

Geometrical Meaning of Rolle's Theorem 
If the graph of a function $y=f\left( x \right)$ be continuous at each point from the point $A\left( a,f\left( a \right) \right)$ to the point $B\left( b,f\left( b \right) \right)$ and tangent at each point between $A$ and $B$ is unique i.e. tangent at each point between A and B exist and ordinates i.e. y co-ordinates of points $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are equal, then there will be at least one point $\text{P}$ on the curve between $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ at which tangent will be parallel to $\text{x}$-axis.

In fig. (i) there is only one such point $\text{P}$, where tangent is parallel to $\text{x}$-axis, but in fig. (ii) there are more than one such points where tangents are parallel to $\text{x}$-axis.

Note : Converse of Rolle's theorem is not true i.e, if a function $f\left( x \right)$ is such that ${f}'\left( c \right)=0$ for at least one $\text{c}$ in the open interval $\left( \text{a},\text{b} \right)$ then it is not necessary that

(i) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is continuous in $\left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$

(ii) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is differentiable in $\left( \text{a},\text{b} \right)$

(iii) $\text{f}\left( \text{a} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{b} \right)$

Algebraic Interpretation of Rolle's Theorem 

For a function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ which is continuous & differentiable and follows Rolle's theorem, i.e. $f\left( a \right)=f\left( b \right)=0$, where $a,b$ are roots of the function then we say there exists $c\in \left( a,b \right)$ such that ${f}'\left( c \right)=0$ or we can say $c$ is the root of ${f}'\left( x \right)$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Between any two roots of a polynomial function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ lies a root of its derivative $\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)$

concepts.title: Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
Lagrange’s Mean Value Theorem: 

For a function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ defined over $\left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$ such that

(i) it is continuous on $\left[ a,b \right]$

(ii) it is differentiable on $\left( a,b \right)$

then there exists a real number $c\in \left( a,b \right)$ such that ${f}'\left( c \right)=\frac{f\left( b \right)-f\left( a \right)}{b-a}$

Note : Rolle Theorem is a special case of this theorem, when $f\left( b \right)=f\left( a \right)$ i.e. ${f}'\left( c \right)=0$

Geometrical Meaning of Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem 

Let $A(a,f\left( a \right)$ and B (b, f (b)) be two points on the curve $y=f\left( x \right)$.

Then $\text{OL}=\text{a},\text{OM}=\text{b},\text{AL}=\text{f}\left( \text{a} \right),\text{BM}=\text{f}\left( \text{b} \right)$.

Now slope of chord $AB$

$\text{tan}\theta =\frac{BK}{AK}=\frac{f\left( b \right)-f\left( a \right)}{b-a}$ …(i)

By Lagrange's Mean Value theorem $\frac{f\left( b \right)-f\left( a \right)}{b-a}={f}'\left( c \right)=$ slope of

tangent at point $\text{P}$ (c, $\text{f}\left( \text{c} \right))$

from (i), $\text{tan}\theta =$ slope of tangent at $P$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ slope of chord $\text{AB}=$ slope of tangent at $\text{P}$

Hence chord $\text{AB}\parallel $ tangent PT.

Thus geometrical meaning of mean value theorem is as follows: 

If the graph of a curve $y=f\left( x \right)$ be continuous at each point from the point $A\left( a,f\left( a \right) \right)$ to to the point $B\left( b,f\left( b \right) \right)$ and tangent at each point between $A$ and $B$ exists i.e. tangent is unique then there will be at least one point $\text{P}$ on the curve between $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$, where tangent will be parallel to chord $\text{AB}$.

In fig. (i) there is only one such point $\text{P}$ where tangent is parallel to chord $\text{AB}$ but in fig. (ii) there are more than one such points where tangents are parallel to chord $\text{AB}$.
